subject: Chemistry
q: An aqueous solution contains an unknown concentration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Ba</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>. When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi><mo> </mo><mi>of</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> is added, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BaSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> just begins to precipitate. The final volume is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math>. The solubility product of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BaSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is the original concentration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Ba</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>?
question: An aqueous solution contains an unknown concentration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Ba</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>. When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi><mo> </mo><mi>of</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> is added, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BaSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> just begins to precipitate. The final volume is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math>. The solubility product of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BaSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is the original concentration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Ba</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>?
solution:
Ba+2+SO42−⇌BaSO4(s)ppt

Final conc. of [SO42−]=MV1V1+V2=1×50500=0.1M

Final conc.of [Ba+2] When BaSO4 start precipitating

KSP=QSP=[Ba+2][SO42−]

10−10=[Ba+2](0.1M)

Initial conc. [Ba+2]; initial volume was =500-50=450 ml
M1V1=M2V2

M1=M2V2V1=10−9×500450

M1=1.1×10−9M

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is strongest Bronsted base?
question: Which of the following is strongest Bronsted base?
solution:
Bronsted base are those which can donate its lone pair to H+ ion. The more easily the compound can donate its lone pair to H+ strong will be a basic character.

Option D, is the strongest Bronsted base due to the availability of a lone pair which does not involve in any resonance.

subject: Chemistry
q: If the solubility product of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>PbS</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>28</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the solubility of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>PbS</mi></math> in pure water at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>298</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>16</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is____ (Nearest integer) [Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>41</mn></math>]
question: If the solubility product of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>PbS</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>28</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the solubility of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>PbS</mi></math> in pure water at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>298</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>16</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is____ (Nearest integer) [Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>41</mn></math>]
solution:
PbSs⇌Pb2+aq+S2-aq

Let us consider the solubility of PbS is "S" M.

Thus,

Solubility product =Pb2+aqS2-aq

Ksp=Pb2+aqS2-aq   ⋯ i

We know

Ksp=S2

S=Ksp=8×10-28=22×10-14

=2.82×10-14

=282×10-16

subject: Chemistry
q: How do we differentiate between $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ in group III?
question: How do we differentiate between $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ in group III?
solution: $\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}$ions are increased to suppress release of $\mathrm{OH}$ ions, hence solubility product of $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_3$ is attained. Colour of precipitate is different.

subject: Chemistry
q:
600mL of 0.01M HCl is mixed with 400mL of 0.01M H2SO4. The pH of the mixture is _____×10-2. (Nearest integer)

[Given log2=0.30,   log3=0.48,  log5=0.69,   log7=0.84,   log11=1.04]
question:
600mL of 0.01M HCl is mixed with 400mL of 0.01M H2SO4. The pH of the mixture is _____×10-2. (Nearest integer)

[Given log2=0.30,   log3=0.48,  log5=0.69,   log7=0.84,   log11=1.04]
solution:
The number of millimoles = Molarity ×  Volume in mL

HCl produce one and H2SO4 produce two H+ ions respectively.

Total milimoles of H+=600×0.01+400×0.01×2=14
Thus, moles of hydrogen ion:
⇒H+=141000=14×10-3
⇒pH = -log[H+]=-log[14 × 10-3]

⇒pH=3-log14

=1.86

=186×10-2

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement-I : Methyl orange is a weak acid.
Statement-II : The benzenoid form of methyl orange is more intense/deeply coloured than the quinonoid form.
In the light of the above statement, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement-I : Methyl orange is a weak acid.
Statement-II : The benzenoid form of methyl orange is more intense/deeply coloured than the quinonoid form.
In the light of the above statement, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution: Methyl orange is a weak acid. Additionally, in acidic conditions, methyl orange exists in the quinonoid form, which is red in color. In alkaline conditions, it exists in the benzenoid form, which is yellow in color. Since red is more deeply coloured than yellow, Statement-2 is wrong.

copper at the cathode

subject: Chemistry
q:
In a sautrated solution of the sparingly soluble strong electrolyte $\mathrm{AgIO}_3$ (Molecular mass $=283$) the equilibrium which sets in is
$\mathrm{AgIO}_{3(\mathrm{s})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{IO}_{3(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}$
If the solubility product constant $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$ of $\mathrm{AgIO}_3$ at a given temperature is $1.0 \times 10^{-8}$, what is the mass of $\mathrm{AgIO}_3$ contained in $100 \mathrm{~ml}$ of its saturated solution?
question:
In a sautrated solution of the sparingly soluble strong electrolyte $\mathrm{AgIO}_3$ (Molecular mass $=283$) the equilibrium which sets in is
$\mathrm{AgIO}_{3(\mathrm{s})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{IO}_{3(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}$
If the solubility product constant $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$ of $\mathrm{AgIO}_3$ at a given temperature is $1.0 \times 10^{-8}$, what is the mass of $\mathrm{AgIO}_3$ contained in $100 \mathrm{~ml}$ of its saturated solution?
solution:
$\mathrm{AgIO}_3(\mathrm{s}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{IO}_3^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$
Let the solubility of $\mathrm{AgIO}_3$ be $\mathrm{s}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{IO}_3^{-}\right]$
$1.0 \times 10^{-8}=\mathrm{s}^2$
$\mathrm{s}=10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{litre}$
$=\frac{10^{-4} \times 283}{1000} \times 100$
$=283 \times 10^{-5}$
$=2.83 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~g} / 100 \mathrm{~ml}$
Hence, (B) is correct.

q: The solubility of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCN</mi></math> in a buffer solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is: [Assume : No cyano complex is formed ; <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>AgCN</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>16</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>HCN</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: The solubility of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCN</mi></math> in a buffer solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is: [Assume : No cyano complex is formed ; <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>AgCN</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>16</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>HCN</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Let, the solubility be x

Given that, 

 KspAgCN=2.2×10-16 and KaHCN=6.2×10-10

AgCN⇌Ag+x+CN-x  Ksp=2.2×10-16

H++CN-⇌HCN  K=1 Ka=16.2×10-10

Ksp×1 Ka=Ag+CN-×[HCN]H+CN-

2.2×10-16×16.2×10-10=[S][S]10-3

S2=2.26.2×10-9

S2=3.55×10-10

S=3.55×10-10

S=1.88×10-5⇒1.9×10-5

subject: Chemistry
q:
The pH of a solution obtained by mixing 50 mL of 1 M HCl and 30 mL of 1 M NaOH is x×10-4. The value of x is (Nearest integer)
log 2.5=0.3979
question:
The pH of a solution obtained by mixing 50 mL of 1 M HCl and 30 mL of 1 M NaOH is x×10-4. The value of x is (Nearest integer)
log 2.5=0.3979
solution:
Milli equivalents of HClNaVa=50×1=50
Milli equivalents of NaOHNbVb=30×1=30
Since NaVa>NbVb
H+=NaVa-NbVbVa+Vb=50-3080=2080=0.25=2.5×10-1
pH=-logH+=-log2.5×10-1=1-0.3979=0.6021
pH×104=0.6021×104=6021

subject: Chemistry
q:
Values of dissociation constant, $K_a$ are given as follows :
$\begin{array}{lc}\text { Acid } & \boldsymbol{K}_{\boldsymbol{a}} \\ \mathrm{HCN} & 6.2 \times 10^{-10} \\ \mathrm{HF} & 7.2 \times 10^{-4} \\ \mathrm{HNO}_2 & 4.0 \times 10^{-4}\end{array}$
Correct order of increasing base strength of the base $\mathrm{CN}^{-}, \mathrm{F}^{-}$and $\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}$will be :
question:
Values of dissociation constant, $K_a$ are given as follows :
$\begin{array}{lc}\text { Acid } & \boldsymbol{K}_{\boldsymbol{a}} \\ \mathrm{HCN} & 6.2 \times 10^{-10} \\ \mathrm{HF} & 7.2 \times 10^{-4} \\ \mathrm{HNO}_2 & 4.0 \times 10^{-4}\end{array}$
Correct order of increasing base strength of the base $\mathrm{CN}^{-}, \mathrm{F}^{-}$and $\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}$will be :
solution:
Higher the value of $\mathrm{K}_a$ lower will be the value of $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ i.e. higher will be the acidic nature. Further since $\mathrm{CN}^{-}, \mathrm{F}^{-}$and $\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}$are conjugate base of the acids $\mathrm{HCN}, \mathrm{HF}$ and $\mathrm{HNO}_2$ respectively hence the correct order of base strength will be
$$
\mathrm{F}^{-} < \mathrm{NO}_2^{-} < \mathrm{CN}^{-}
$$
$(\because$ stronger the acid weaker will be its conjugate base)

subject: Chemistry
q:
A solution is 0.1M in Cl- and 0.001M in CrO42-.
Solid AgNO3 is gradually added to it Assuming that the addition does not change in volume and Ksp(AgCl)=1.7×10-10M2 and
KspAg2CrO4=1.9×10-12M3.

Select correct statement from the following:
question:
A solution is 0.1M in Cl- and 0.001M in CrO42-.
Solid AgNO3 is gradually added to it Assuming that the addition does not change in volume and Ksp(AgCl)=1.7×10-10M2 and
KspAg2CrO4=1.9×10-12M3.

Select correct statement from the following:
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math> precipitates first because its <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub></math> is high.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ag</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CrO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> precipitates first as its <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub></math> is low.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ag</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CrO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> precipitates first because the amount of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Ag</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> needed is low.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math> will precipitate first as the amount of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Ag</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> needed to precipitate is low.
solution:
i Ag+ required to ppt AgCls

Ksp=IP=Ag+Cl-=1.7×10-10

Ag+=1.7×10-9

ii Ag+ required to ppt Ag2CrO4s

Ksp=IP=[Ag+]2CrO4-2=1.9×10-12

Ag+=4.3×10-5

Ag+ required to ppt AgCl is low so AgCl will ppt 1st.
